_comment: Box Office entitlement token test vectors. Generated by a throwaway ES256 test keypair (scripts/gen-test-vectors.js is NOT committed -- it lives only in the generating session's scratch dir). Phase 1 regenerates this file with the REAL signing key once SETUP.md step S11 exists, and re-commits this exact file to both repos byte-identical.
publicKeyFormat: X.509 SubjectPublicKeyInfo, DER, base64-encoded -- parseable directly via Kotlin's X509EncodedKeySpec / KeyFactory.getInstance("EC")
algorithm: ES256 (SHA256withECDSA), signature encoded as raw R||S (64 bytes for P-256), base64url, per RFC 7518 JWS -- NOT DER in the token itself; the verifier converts raw R||S to DER internally before calling java.security.Signature.verify()

vectors.name: valid
vectors.description: A correctly signed, unexpired token with full Unlimited entitlements.

vectors.name: expired
vectors.description: Correctly signed but exp is in the past.

vectors.name: bad_signature
vectors.description: Valid structure and claims, but the signature has been tampered with (one character flipped).

vectors.name: wrong_kid
vectors.description: Correctly signed with the real test key, but the header's kid does not match any pinned public key.

vectors.name: malformed_ent
vectors.description: ent claim is not a valid Entitlements object (wrong JSON shape entirely).

vectors.name: forward_compatible
vectors.description: The single most important vector: ent carries an unknown future capability (canCastSync) AND omits a known one (canExportCast). The verifier must accept and parse this token (ignoring canCastSync), and the resulting Entitlements.canExportCast must resolve to false (fail-closed), never throw and never default to true.

vectors.name: sunset_valid
vectors.description: A genuine sunset declaration, signed with the real (pinned) key. No exp claim by design.

vectors.name: sunset_wrong_key
vectors.description: A forged sunset declaration: claims the real kid in its header but is actually signed with a different (attacker) private key. Must be rejected -- this is the case that proves the sunset mechanism can't be spoofed without the real private key.
